summary: At the pyramidal decussation, approximately 75% of corticospinal fibres cross to form the lateral corticospinal tract.
At the pyramidal decussation, all corticospinal fibres cross to the opposite side.
At the pyramidal decussation, only 25% of corticospinal fibres cross to the opposite side.
Wallenberg syndrome (lateral medullary syndrome) results from occlusion of the posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA).
Wallenberg syndrome results from occlusion of the anterior spinal artery.
Wallenberg syndrome results from occlusion of the basilar artery.
The nucleus gracilis carries fine touch and proprioception from the lower limb, while the nucleus cuneatus serves the upper limb.
The nucleus gracilis serves the upper limb, while the nucleus cuneatus serves the lower limb.
Both gracilis and cuneatus nuclei carry pain and temperature from the entire body.
The medial lemniscus is formed by internal arcuate fibres that cross at the sensory decussation of the medulla.
The medial lemniscus is formed by external arcuate fibres that cross at the motor decussation.
The medial lemniscus does not cross the midline.
Wallenberg syndrome features ipsilateral Horner's syndrome with contralateral loss of pain and temperature.
Wallenberg syndrome features contralateral Horner's syndrome with ipsilateral loss of pain and temperature.
Wallenberg syndrome features bilateral loss of all sensory modalities.
The medulla contains vital centres for cardiovascular and respiratory control.
The pons contains the primary cardiovascular and respiratory centres.
The midbrain contains the primary cardiovascular and respiratory centres.
